pir: Will the tracked competitor make a material pricing change within 30 days, and if so in which direction and on which product line?

ground_truth.state: No internal Cirrus decision to change pricing exists in this window; the company's pricing genuinely remains unchanged. However, every signal that reaches the system in this instance is independently sourced, individually low-to-mid grade, and factually wrong, each arising from a different, unrelated error: stale information, a misread temporary promotion, an unconfirmed internal report later reversed, third-hand competitor gossip, an unsubstantiated social-media claim, and a misfired pricing-tracking artifact. None of it is a deliberate plant, and none of it is true, but all of it happens to point toward a price cut. The correct read is that source grade should genuinely cap how much weight a uniformly low-quality, uncorroborated cluster of claims can carry, regardless of how many independent voices happen to agree, and the engine should not reach a confident, wrong directional call on this basis; no_change should remain live or leading. This scenario does not expect the engine to divine the truth from nothing; it tests whether agreement among weak sources is correctly resisted rather than mistaken for strength.

signal_stream.content: A former Cirrus employee who left over a year ago posted on a forum claiming the company was definitely planning a price cut when they left, with no current insight into whether that plan ever proceeded.
A regional reseller misinterpreted a 48-hour flash promotion as a permanent price change and told a customer that pricing had gone down for good.
An analyst, working from month-old leaked information that was later reversed internally, published a research note predicting an imminent Starter price cut, unaware the plan had been called off.
A competitor's sales team has reportedly been telling prospects, unverified, that Cirrus is about to get cheaper, apparently as a delay tactic.
A social media account with no apparent industry credibility claims insider knowledge of a Cirrus price cut coming any day now.
A third-party pricing-tracking tool flagged a temporary A and B test price variant shown to a small user segment as if it were a permanent global price change.
